success_surface: Cell-Level Formula Correction with Dynamic Row Application
effective_behaviors: Two-phase execution: explore schema first with pandas, then modify with openpyxl for formula support
Dynamic row-range detection using workbook metadata instead of hardcoded values
Template-based formula generation with row placeholders for batch application
Pre-save verification of modified cells to confirm formula syntax correctness
Proper workbook lifecycle management: explicit load, modification, save, and close
evidence: Agent first loaded sheet with pandas.read_excel() to inspect columns and data shape
Used ws.max_row to determine actual data extent before applying formulas
Created formula template '=IF(B{row}<37.5,D{row}*2,D{row}*1.5)' with dynamic row formatting
Verified updated cells at indices [2, 5, 10, 15] showed correct formula strings after assignment
Closed workbook explicitly with wb.close() after saving output

memory_items.title: Hybrid Tool Workflow
memory_items.lesson: Use pandas for quick schema exploration and openpyxl for formula-level modifications.
memory_items.generalization: When task requires both data understanding and formula manipulation, split workflow: pandas for inspection, openpyxl for cell value/formula changes.

memory_items.title: Dynamic Range Determination
memory_items.lesson: Derive row/column ranges from workbook metadata rather than hardcoding.
memory_items.generalization: Always query ws.max_row, max_column, or relevant headers to determine operation scope dynamically.

memory_items.title: Batch Formula Generation Pattern
memory_items.lesson: Build formula templates with row placeholders for efficient batch updates.
memory_items.generalization: For multi-row formula insertion, create format strings with row indices and iterate rather than setting each cell individually.

memory_items.title: Verification Before Commitment
memory_items.lesson: Sample-verify formula assignments before saving workbook.
memory_items.generalization: After bulk cell modifications, spot-check several affected cells to confirm expected values or formulas were written correctly.

memory_items.title: Excel Formula Syntax Discipline
memory_items.lesson: Write formulas using standard Excel notation when assigning via code.
memory_items.generalization: All programmatic formula assignments must use valid Excel syntax (operators, no string quoting around numeric literals).

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Hybrid Tool Workflow
- For tasks requiring both data understanding and formula manipulation, split the workflow:
  1. Use `pandas` for quick schema inspection (column names, data types, row counts)
  2. Switch to `openpyxl` for formula-level cell modifications where calculation logic matters

### Dynamic Range Determination
- Always derive operation scope from workbook metadata (`ws.max_row`, `max_column`, header queries) rather than hardcoding row numbers or ranges
- This ensures scripts adapt to varying dataset sizes without modification

### Batch Formula Generation Pattern
- When inserting formulas across multiple rows, build a format string template with row placeholders (e.g., `"=IF(B{row}..."`)
- Iterate through detected range and apply formatted template to each target cell
- More efficient than setting each cell's formula individually

### Verification Before Commitment
- After bulk cell modifications, spot-check several randomly selected affected cells
- Confirm formula strings or calculated values match expectations before saving
- Catch syntax errors early in the modification loop

### Excel Formula Syntax Discipline
- All programmatic formula assignments must use valid Excel formula syntax
- Avoid Python string artifacts: no quotes around numeric literals, use `*` for multiplication not `"*"`
- Standard notation: `=A1+B1`, `=IF(cond,value1,value2)`
